planting.note: Direct sow in fall (September–November in zone 9) for winter establishment and spring bloom; in zone 9 fall sowing is far more successful than spring sowing. Myosotis sylvatica is a cool-season annual that germinates readily in cool soil but struggles in warm-season conditions. Indigo Blue produces the deepest, most saturated blue available in the Myosotis sylvatica color range — a true indigo-blue rather than the lighter sky-blue of standard selections. Partial shade performance is genuine — forget-me-nots grow naturally in shaded woodland settings and perform better in partial shade in zone 9 than in full sun, which causes premature wilting and earlier decline. Self-seeds prolifically in zone 9 cool-season garden beds, creating a naturalizing population that reappears each fall from seed.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun — partial shade is preferred in zone 9 where the cool, moist, shaded conditions of the natural forest understory habitat are approximated under deciduous trees — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Indigo Blue Forget-Me-Not is a deep-colored selection of Woodland Forget-Me-Not (Myosotis sylvatica), producing the characteristic tiny, five-petaled, sky-to-indigo blue flowers with a small yellow eye in dense clusters on low, branching stems — creating a spreading mat of blue bloom that is one of the most universally recognized and beloved cool-season garden flowers. The characteristic tiny blue flowers of forget-me-not have been celebrated in poetry, art, and sentiment for centuries — the simple, vivid blue of the flowers carrying a disproportionate emotional resonance compared to the diminutive scale of individual blooms. As a woodland native, Myosotis sylvatica genuinely tolerates and prefers partial shade conditions — growing naturally beneath deciduous trees where it benefits from winter sun through bare branches and then spring dappled light through emerging leaves. In zone 9 forget-me-nots provide a solid carpet of blue in January–March, naturalizing through self-seeding in appropriate cool, moist, partially shaded sites to create an ever-expanding spring wildflower planting requiring no annual replanting.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Shade Annuals for Gulf South
University of Georgia Extension — Myosotis sylvatica for Southern Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Forget-Me-Not for Florida Winter Annual Gardens

planting.note: Start seeds indoors in late summer (August–September in zone 9) for fall transplanting, or plant purchased transplants in October–November. Pomponette is a double-flowered English Daisy selection producing button-like, fully double, rounded flower heads in pink, red, and white — one of the most charming and old-fashioned cool-season bedding plant flowers available. In zone 9 Bellis perennis blooms November–April in an essentially continuous display in appropriate shade and moisture conditions. English daisy performs better in partial shade in zone 9 than in full sun, which causes premature wilting and earlier heat decline. As a technically perennial species (Bellis perennis = perennial daisy), it may persist through mild zone 9 summers in cool, moist, shaded situations but is most reliably treated as a cool-season annual.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun — partial shade is preferred in zone 9 where mild winter temperatures and early spring warmth can cause premature heat stress in fully exposed sites — in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Pomponette English Daisy is a double-flowered selection of English Daisy (Bellis perennis), the lawn daisy of European meadows and cottage gardens, producing small, fully double, button-like flower heads in pink, rose-red, and white on compact plants — creating an old-fashioned, charming, cottage garden effect that is entirely different from the more modern, large-flowered cool-season annuals that have come to dominate the bedding plant market. The completely double, pompom-form flowers of Pomponette — no visible center disc, entirely composed of tightly packed, inward-curving ray petals — provide an unusual texture compared to other cool-season daisies, producing a more formal, tidier flower form at a compact scale. English daisy's genuine tolerance for partial shade — as a species native to European woodlands and meadows where it grows in both open and lightly shaded conditions — makes it one of the few compact, low-growing cool-season flowering annuals that can deliver consistent bloom in shaded winter and spring gardens in zone 9. The low, compact form (6–8 inches) works particularly well as an edging plant with taller cool-season companions such as snapdragons, pansies, and alyssum.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Shade Annuals for Gulf South
University of Georgia Extension — Bellis perennis for Southern Winter Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — English Daisy for Florida Cool-Season Shade Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (October–November in zone 9) for winter establishment and spring bloom; poppies do not transplant well and must be direct-sown. Cool soil (50–65°F) is required for germination — do not bury seed; press lightly into the soil surface or rake in shallowly. In zone 9 fall sowing produces the best plants and bloom; spring sowing in warming soil produces poor results. Shirley Poppy was developed by Reverend Wilks in Shirley, England in the 1880s by selecting from wild Corn Poppy — the Shirley strains producing single and semi-double flowers with translucent, tissue-thin petals in pink, red, rose, salmon, and white, typically with a white center ring. Plants self-seed readily in zone 9 to naturalize in sandy, well-drained soils. All parts of Papaver rhoeas are mildly toxic.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil — Shirley Poppies demand full sun and poor to moderately fertile, lean soil; in rich, fertilized soil they produce excessive foliage with reduced flowering; Shirley Poppy Mix is a classic selection of Corn Poppy (Papaver rhoeas), producing the tissue-thin, crinkled-petal, bowl-shaped flowers in an extended color range of pink, rose, salmon, red, and white — often with picotee edges, darker veining, or a distinctive white base ring — that have made Shirley poppies one of the most beloved and evocative of all annual wildflowers for the cottage and cutting garden. The distinctive visual quality of Shirley poppy flowers — the tissue-paper-thin petals catching and transmitting light, creating a slightly translucent, glowing quality particularly effective in morning and evening light — is entirely different from the opaque, solid color of most other annual flowers, producing an effect of delicacy and refinement at odds with the plant's actually tough, drought-tolerant character. Corn Poppy (Papaver rhoeas) is native to the Mediterranean and Middle East and has been associated with remembrance, sleep, and beauty in European and Mediterranean culture for thousands of years — the simple, vivid flowers carrying cultural weight disproportionate to the plant's modest agricultural weed origins. In zone 9 fall-sown Shirley poppies bloom in February–April in a naturalistic wildflower display that self-renews through seed-drop each year once established.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Fall-Sown Wildflower Annuals for Gulf South
University of Georgia Extension — Direct-Sow Cool-Season Annuals for Southern Gardens
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Papaver rhoeas for Southern Wildflower Gardens

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or plant transplants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. The Talent series is one of the most popular silver-leaved gazania strains — the silvery-white, pubescent foliage providing an additional ornamental dimension beyond the vivid orange flowers. Gazania flowers open fully only in sun and close on cloudy days and at night. The plants are highly drought-tolerant once established and perform best in lean, well-drained, sandy soils — excessive moisture or fertility causes root rot and reduced flowering. Gazania is borderline perennial in zone 9 — the plants may survive mild winters as semi-woody perennials but are most reliably grown as warm-season annuals. The orange flowers provide strong warm-color contrast with blue and purple companion plants.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Gazania requires full sun (flowers close in shade) and excellent drainage; it thrives in hot, dry, coastal conditions where many other annuals decline; Talent Orange Gazania is a silver-leaved selection of Treasure Flower (Gazania rigens), a South African perennial grown as a warm-season annual — producing vivid orange, large (3-inch), single daisy flowers with darker orange or brown striping at the base of the ray petals that creates a striped, star-patterned floral display above a low, spreading rosette of distinctive silver-gray, deeply divided leaves. The silver-white foliage of the Talent series is one of the most striking and unusual features of this selection — the densely pubescent leaf surface covering the leaves in fine silver hairs that give the entire plant a silvery, reflective quality, adding a year-round foliage interest that makes the plant attractive even when not in bloom. Gazania's South African coastal origin (fynbos and coastal scrub) gives it exceptional adaptability to hot, dry, sandy, salt-air conditions that are common in Gulf Coast home gardens — making it one of the most practical ornamental annuals for beach-adjacent and coastal zone 9 gardens where other annuals struggle with salt, sand, and periodic drought. The combination of vivid orange flowers, attractive silver foliage, extreme drought and heat tolerance, and compact mounding habit makes Talent Orange one of the most useful full-sun, low-water annuals for zone 9.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gazania for Florida Annual Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Drought-Tolerant South African Annuals for Zone 9
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Annuals for Gulf South Dry Sites

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Cora is a vegetatively propagated vinca series from Syngenta Flowers — one of the highest-performing Catharanthus series for heat, humidity, and Phytophthora blight resistance. The Cora series is widely used in commercial landscaping and public plantings throughout the Gulf South precisely because of its superior disease resistance and longer season performance compared to seed-grown vinca series. Cora Strawberry produces vivid strawberry-red to rose-red flowers with a white central eye on plants with notably glossy, dark green foliage — the glossy foliage being a distinguishing trait of the Cora series that provides additional ornamental value between flower cycles.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with consistent moisture during establishment — Cora Strawberry Vinca is extremely heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant once established, requiring minimal water and no deadheading to maintain continuous bloom; Cora Strawberry Vinca is a premium, vegetatively propagated Catharanthus roseus cultivar producing vivid strawberry-red flowers with a white central eye on compact, well-branched, glossy-leaved plants — one of the highest-rated commercial vinca series for disease resistance, heat performance, and season-long ornamental quality in the Gulf South landscape. The Cora series distinguishes itself from seed-grown vinca series through consistently higher resistance to Phytophthora crown rot — the primary disease that limits annual vinca performance in humid zone 9 conditions — and through the notably glossy, dark green foliage that remains attractive throughout the season regardless of flowering intensity. Catharanthus roseus (annual vinca, Madagascar periwinkle) is the quintessential Gulf South summer annual — heat-thriving, drought-tolerant, self-cleaning, and maintaining its bloom display through the peak summer conditions of July–September that challenge most other annual species. The vivid strawberry-red with white eye of Cora Strawberry provides one of the most visually energetic bicolor combinations available in the vinca color range — the strawberry-red and white creating a lively, high-contrast display effective in both formal mass planting and mixed container compositions.
sources: Syngenta Flowers — Cora Series Catharanthus Performance Data
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Vinca (Catharanthus roseus) Disease Resistance
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Best Vinca Series for Gulf South Summer Performance
